findings.title: WorkflowCommander fetch parses response incorrectly — data contract mismatch
findings.description: The /api/workflows endpoint returns `{ workflows: WorkflowMeta[], version: string }`, but WorkflowCommander.svelte directly assigns the response as WorkflowMeta[]. This means `workflows` will always be empty or contain the wrong shape.
findings.impact: Workflow card list will never render correctly — zero visible workflows for the user.
findings.suggestion: Change to `.then((data: { workflows: WorkflowMeta[] }) => { workflows = data.workflows; ... })`

findings.title: stores/index.ts import path for WSClient is broken
findings.description: The import path resolves correctly relative to the file location. However, stores/index.ts uses `derived(connectionWritable, ...)` wrapping a polling interval to sync WSClient's $state runes into a Svelte store — the pattern is unconventional but functional. VERIFIED: path '../services/ws-client.js' from 'src/lib/client/stores/index.ts' correctly resolves to 'src/lib/client/services/ws-client.ts'. The verification.json GAP-001 appears to be a false positive from an earlier state.
findings.impact: None — import path is correct. The polling pattern introduces slight latency (500ms max) in connection state updates.
findings.suggestion: Consider replacing 500ms polling with $effect-based reactivity in Svelte 5 if possible.

findings.title: +page.svelte creates subscription leak in $effect
findings.description: $effect() runs on every re-render and each invocation creates a new subscription via connectionState.subscribe() without unsubscribing the previous one. This causes subscription accumulation / memory leak.
findings.impact: Memory leak on every reactive cycle; `connected` callback fires N times after N cycles.
findings.suggestion: Use `$effect` with cleanup: `$effect(() => { const unsub = connectionState.subscribe(s => { connected = s.connected; }); return unsub; });` Or better: use `$derived` or direct store auto-subscription `$connectionState.connected`.

findings.title: stores/index.ts creates infinite reconnection loop
findings.description: Subscribing to connectionState and calling wsClient.connect() whenever disconnected creates an infinite loop: disconnect → subscribe fires → connect() → onclose fires → connected=false → subscribe fires → connect() again, ignoring the WSClient's own exponential backoff.
findings.impact: Bypasses WSClient's reconnect delay, potentially flooding the server with rapid connection attempts. Also races with the internal reconnect timer.
findings.suggestion: Remove this subscription. WSClient already has its own reconnection logic with exponential backoff (scheduleReconnect). Add a one-time connect call in an `onMount` or top-level initialization instead.

findings.title: POST /api/workflows/execute has no input validation
findings.snippet: const body = await c.req.json<{ workflowId: string; params: Record<string, unknown> }>();
findings.description: The workflowId string is passed directly to buildPrompt which feeds into `spawn('maestro', ['delegate', prompt, ...])`. While `spawn` with array args doesn't allow shell injection, a crafted workflowId containing malicious content could be interpreted by the `maestro delegate` CLI's prompt parser.
findings.impact: Potential CLI argument injection via crafted workflowId/params values. Severity mitigated because spawn uses array form (no shell expansion).
findings.suggestion: Add input validation: validate workflowId is a known workflow name (alphanumeric + dashes), validate params keys/values. Consider a whitelist approach.

findings.title: GET /api/project/state uses string concatenation for file path
findings.description: MAESTRO_PROJECT_DIR is read from environment without sanitization. If set to a malicious path (e.g., containing `../`), it could read arbitrary files. Since this is a local dev tool with env controlled by the user, risk is low.
findings.impact: Low — path traversal only possible if attacker controls MAESTRO_PROJECT_DIR env var.
findings.suggestion: Use `path.resolve(projectDir, '.workflow', 'state.json')` and optionally verify it's within an expected directory. Import `path` from `node:path`.

findings.title: stores/index.ts polls WSClient state every 500ms
findings.description: A 500ms polling interval to bridge $state runes to Svelte stores is wasteful. Connection state changes are infrequent (connect/disconnect), so most polls do nothing. The interval is never cleared.
findings.impact: Minor CPU overhead; never cleans up on component unmount or SSR.
findings.suggestion: Replace with event-driven approach: have WSClient emit events or use a callback pattern. Alternatively, accept $state as the source of truth if Svelte 5 allows direct reactivity without stores.

findings.title: EventBus history slicing creates new array on every overflow
findings.description: When history exceeds 1000 entries, a new 1000-element array is allocated. For high-throughput scenarios this could cause GC pressure.
findings.impact: Minor — only triggers when buffer is full, and 1000 events is relatively small.
findings.suggestion: Consider using a ring buffer or splice-based approach: `this.history.shift()` per event, or a circular buffer with a head pointer.

findings.title: Missing src/app.css referenced by +layout.svelte
findings.description: The layout imports app.css which does not exist on disk. This would cause a build-time error in production, though SvelteKit's dev server may silently ignore it.
findings.impact: Build will fail in production/CI. No global CSS reset or base styles loaded.
findings.suggestion: Create `src/app.css` with a CSS reset and base variables (font, box-sizing, etc.), or remove the import.

findings.title: Server singleton export pattern conflicts with testability
findings.description: A module-level singleton EventBus is exported but MaestroIDEServer creates its own instances via constructor injection. The singleton is unused in production code but could cause confusion — tests or other modules might import the singleton instead of the injected instance.
findings.impact: Potential subtle bugs if a consumer imports the singleton instead of using the DI instance.
findings.suggestion: Remove the singleton export, or document it as deprecated. All production usage should go through MaestroIDEServer.eventBus.

findings.title: No error typing on catch blocks
findings.description: Using `any` type on catch blocks (only one instance here) bypasses TypeScript's strict typing. This is a common pattern but `unknown` is preferred.
findings.impact: Minor — reduces type safety in error handling paths.
findings.suggestion: Use `catch (err: unknown)` and type-narrow: `if (err instanceof Error && 'code' in err) { ... }`

findings.title: WorkflowCommander retry handler is inline and duplicates fetch logic
findings.snippet: onclick={() => { loading = true; error = null; fetch('/api/workflows').then(...) }}
findings.description: The retry button inline handler duplicates the initial fetch logic from $effect. Should be extracted into a shared function.
findings.impact: DRY violation — if fetch logic changes, two places need updating.
findings.suggestion: Extract `loadWorkflows()` function and call it from both `$effect` and the retry handler.
